metadata.title: Build the 10-Minute Presentation
metadata.playlist: Make It Count — The Complete Brand System
metadata.source: INFO 7375 Capstone — Complete Brand System (200 pts, due 2026-08-14)

beats.narration_text: Ciao — this is Liam. Episode 4 of 6. Great work presented badly still loses. The deck is not decoration for what you built — it is the vehicle that makes the grader understand it in ten minutes. Build it to the exact time budget, in the exact pro structure, with five sections that each have a job and a clock. This episode walks each section: what goes in, how much time it gets, and what mistake kills each one. Let's go.

props.command: Build my capstone deck to the exact section budgets. Brand system pasted below.
props.runningText: great work presented badly still loses — five sections, exact time budget, each one has a job…
output: Five sections named with time budgets
The mistake that kills each section

beats.narration_text: Section 1: Brand Introduction. One minute. One slide, maybe two. Your name. Your positioning statement — the one from the strategy document, stated exactly, not paraphrased or softened. And one brand essence moment: the color, the logo, the one image or phrase that makes a stranger understand what kind of brand this is in three seconds. The killer mistake in this section is starting with backstory. Do not explain how you got here, do not thank the class, do not build to the positioning — state the positioning first. Name, positioning statement, essence. The grader has been in the room for seven presentations before yours. Your first fifteen seconds either orient them or lose them. Start with the brand, not with you.

artifactLines: Slide: name + positioning statement (from the strategy doc — stated exactly, not softened).
Brand essence: the color, mark, or phrase that makes the brand legible in 3 seconds.
Killer mistake: starting with backstory or 'how I got here.'
State the positioning first — the grader has heard 7 presentations before yours.
First 15 seconds: orient or lose. Start with the brand, not with you.
props.sparkLine: Name. Positioning. Essence. One minute. Nothing else.

beats.narration_text: Sections 2 and 3: Strategy and Visual Identity. Two minutes each. Section 2, Strategy: audience description, UVP stated in full, brand pillars listed, competitive positioning explained. Not read from a slide — presented. The grader is listening for whether you understand your own strategy or are reciting it. If you cannot explain why you chose this audience or what makes your positioning different from a generic claim, the section fails regardless of what the slide says. Section 3, Visual Identity: logo variations with a brief explanation of when each is used, palette shown with hex values visible, type samples side by side, a short website walkthrough, and one or two social examples. The walkthrough is not a tour — it is one click path that demonstrates the brand in context. The killer mistake in Section 3 is reading the style guide aloud. Show the brand working — do not explain what color your primary button is.

props.artifactTitle: Sections 2–3: Strategy + Visual Identity — 2 min each
props.artifactHeading: Strategy: present it. Identity: show it working.
artifactLines: Strategy (2 min): audience · UVP in full · pillars listed · competitive positioning — presented, not recited.
Test: can you explain WHY this audience and WHY this positioning without reading the slide?
Visual Identity (2 min): logo variations + use cases · hex palette visible · type samples · site walkthrough · social.
Walkthrough = one click path showing brand in context — not a tour of every page.
Killer mistake: reading the style guide aloud. Show the brand working.
props.sparkLine: Strategy: know it. Identity: show it working. Both in 2 minutes each.

beats.narration_text: Section 4: Brand Storytelling. Two minutes. One story, told in full. Not summarized, not referenced — told. The story arc played out from opening to turning point to outcome. Then: name the framework you used and explain why you chose it for this particular story. Not 'I used the Hero's Journey' as a label — explain the fit: 'I chose Hero's Journey because the transformation I was describing needed a before and after, and the arc gave the grader a place to locate the change.' Then connect the story to a specific brand value — not a general theme, a specific pillar from the strategy document. The killer mistake in this section is presenting a story summary instead of the story. A summary is 'I had a challenge, I overcame it, I learned something.' A story has the specific situation, the specific decision, the specific outcome that changed something. The summary can be stated in fifteen seconds. The story takes the two minutes. Use the two minutes.

props.artifactHeading: One story in full · name the framework · connect to a pillar
artifactLines: Tell ONE story in full — not summarized, not referenced. Opening → turning point → outcome.
Name the framework AND explain the fit: why did this arc suit this story?
Connect to a specific brand pillar from the strategy doc — not a general theme.
Killer mistake: presenting a summary ('I had a challenge, I overcame it') instead of the story.
A summary takes 15 seconds. The story takes 2 minutes. Use the 2 minutes.
props.sparkLine: Tell the story. Name the framework. Connect to a pillar. In that order.

beats.narration_text: Section 5: Tool Demo. Three minutes — the largest single section. Start with a framing slide, not with the tool running. One slide, fifteen seconds, that tells the grader what they are about to see: what the tool does, who it is for, and what the methodology is. Then live. Two or three features, in the order that tells the story of the tool's value — not the order they appear in the navigation. The first feature should be the one that makes the value obvious. The second should be the one that makes the methodology visible. The third, if you have one, should be the one that shows depth. The live-means-live framing from Episode 2 applies here: the demo runs live unless it fails, in which case you switch to the recorded fallback without apologizing. The killer mistake in this section is spending more than thirty seconds on any feature that is not demonstrating value. The grader is not interested in your loading state. Keep the demo moving.

props.artifactHeading: Framing slide first · then live · 2–3 features in value order
artifactLines: Framing slide (15 sec): what it does · who it's for · methodology — before touching the tool.
Feature order: value obvious (1st) · methodology visible (2nd) · depth (3rd if applicable).
Live means live: if demo fails, switch to recorded fallback without apologizing.
Killer mistake: >30 sec on any feature that isn't demonstrating value.
The grader is not interested in your loading state. Keep the demo moving.
props.sparkLine: Frame it first. Then live. Value obvious → methodology visible → depth.

beats.narration_text: Verdict for Build the 10-Minute Presentation. Five sections, five time budgets, each with a job and a killer mistake. Brand Intro: one minute, positioning first, no backstory. Strategy: two minutes, presented not recited — know why you made each choice. Visual Identity: two minutes, show the brand working, do not read the style guide. Brand Storytelling: two minutes, one story told in full, named framework with a fit explanation, connected to a specific pillar. Tool Demo: three minutes, framing slide first, features in value order, live with a fallback ready. Total: ten minutes. Target: nine thirty. Episode 5 is the rehearsal.

props.artifactHeading: Five sections · five budgets · five killer mistakes
artifactLines: Brand Intro (1 min): positioning first — no backstory.
Strategy (2 min): presented, not recited — know why each choice.
Visual Identity (2 min): show the brand working — don't read the style guide.
Brand Storytelling (2 min): one story in full · named framework + fit · specific pillar.
Tool Demo (3 min): framing slide · value order · live + fallback. Target: 9:30.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Use Claude to generate the deck structure before you open any slide software. Paste this prompt in with your brand system details filled in. The prompt reads: I am building the 10-minute capstone presentation for INFO 7375. I need you to generate the complete deck structure with section-by-section slide outlines, following the exact time budgets. Here is my brand system. Brand name: [name]. Positioning statement: [paste exactly from strategy doc]. Audience: [describe]. Brand pillars: [list 3–5]. Competitive positioning: [describe in one sentence]. Logo variations: [list what you have]. Palette hex values: [list]. Primary and secondary type: [names and weights]. Website URL: [paste]. Brand story I will present: [paste the story in full — opening, turning point, outcome]. Framework I used for that story: [name + one sentence on why you chose it]. Brand pillar this story connects to: [name]. Tool name and description: [describe]. Two or three demo features in the order I plan to present them: [list]. Recorded fallback available: [yes or no]. With this information, build the slide-by-slide outline for all five sections, with slide count, estimated time per slide, and the specific content for each slide. Flag any section likely to run over its budget based on what I have given you. After you get the outline, build the slides to match it — do not improvise the structure during the rehearsal.

props.command: Brand name · positioning statement · audience · pillars · competitive positioning · logo/palette/type · website URL · brand story (full) + framework + pillar · tool description · 2–3 demo features in order · fallback? Build slide-by-slide outline for 5 sections to exact time budgets. Flag any section likely to overrun.
props.runningText: build the slides to match the outline — don't improvise the structure during rehearsal…

beats.narration_text: Build the 10-Minute Presentation. Liam, in for Bear.
